data.title: Liberty Energy jumps after Q1 profit topper, expects sequential revenue growth
data.description:
Liberty Energy (NYSE:LBRT) +9.5% in Thursday's trading after reporting better than expected Q1 GAAP earnings and revenues, and saying it expects sequential growth in revenue and profitability in Q2 due to higher utilization.

Q1 net income fell to $20M from $82M in Q1 2024 and $52M in Q4 2024, while Q1 adjusted EBITDA of $168M fell 31% from $245M in the same quarter of 2024 but increased 8% from $156M in Q4 2024.

Current levels of fracking activity suggest U.S. oil production will hold steady, "mitigating the possibility of steep declines experienced by the service industry in past cycles," Liberty (NYSE:LBRT) CEO Ron Gusek said.

"Our early year results demonstrate a positive rebound from the fourth quarter of 2024, a trend that has continued into the second quarter," CEO Ron Gusek said. "While North American producers have not yet meaningfully changed development plans, we expect our customers to assess a range of scenarios in anticipation of commodity price pressure."

Liberty (NYSE:LBRT) is the first major U.S.-based oil service company to post quarterly results, with Halliburton scheduled to follow Tuesday.

data.title: IHS Markit slashes global growth outlook amid mounting investor jitters
data.description:
Ongoing trade tensions and related uncertainties are casting a long shadow over global financial markets, leading to continued volatility and growing investor unease IHS Markit said. Equity markets have seen pronounced corrections since the April 2 announcement of reciprocal tariffs, with only modest and short-lived recoveries following a temporary pause in trade measures on April 9.

In response to these developments, IHS Markit has revised its global economic outlook downward, stating “we have cut our global growth forecasts for 2025 and 2026 in April’s update, reﬂecting the issues above.” 

The firm’s base case now sees global real GDP growth at 2.2% for 2025, down from the previous forecast of 2.5%. For 2026, it projects growth of 2.4%, a reduction from its earlier estimate of 2.7%. These figures mark the weakest projected growth rates since the 2008–09 financial crisis, excluding the pandemic years. 

Adding to investor concerns is the limited expected monetary policy response. IHS Markit indicated that it anticipates only modest easing by the U.S. Federal Reserve this year, reflecting persistent inflationary pressures and uncertainty about the broader economic trajectory. 

IHS Markit finished by saying downside risks remain significant, and markets are likely to remain turbulent in the months ahead. As a result, risk aversion remains elevated, and market conditions are choppy. 

Market Tracking ETFs: (NYSEARCA:DIA), (NYSEARCA:DDM), (NYSEARCA:DOG), (NYSEARCA:DXD), (NYSEARCA:SDOW), (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), (NYSEARCA:SPXU), (NASDAQ:QQQ), (NASDAQ:QQQM), (NYSEARCA:QLD), (NASDAQ:TQQQ), (NYSEARCA:QID), and (NASDAQ:SQQQ). 

Fixed Income Tracking ETFs: (NASDAQ:TLT), (NYSEARCA:TLH), (NASDAQ:IEF), (NASDAQ:IEI), (NASDAQ:SHY), (NYSEARCA:SGOV), (NYSEARCA:SCHO), (NYSEARCA:BIL), (NYSEARCA:AGG), (NASDAQ:BND), (NASDAQ:VCIT), (NYSEARCA:MUB), (NASDAQ:MBB), (NYSEARCA:JNK), (NYSEARCA:LQD), (NYSEARCA:HYG), and (NYSEARCA:TIP).

data.title: Infosys to acquire cybersecurity company, The Missing Link
data.description:
• Infosys (NYSE:INFY) announced on Thursday a definitive agreement to acquire The Missing Link, an Australian cybersecurity services specialist firm with capabilities across the full stack of cyber solutions.
  • This strategic investment further strengthens Infosys' cybersecurity capabilities, while bolstering its presence in the fast-growing Australian market, the company said.
  • The acquisition is expected to close during the first quarter of fiscal year 2026.
  • Infosys (INFY) shares were last down 2.29%.

data.title: Dow drops nearly 500 points on UnitedHealth slump as Wall Street heads for a weekly loss
data.description:
Wall Street on Thursday was mixed, in part due to a post-earnings slump in industry bellwether UnitedHealth (UNH). It is a holiday-shortened week, with markets shut on Good Friday. 

Monetary policy was also in the spotlight after U.S. President Donald Trump criticized Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell. Separately, the European Central Bank (ECB) cut interest rates for the seventh time in a year, and said that the outlook for growth had "deteriorated owing to rising trade tensions."

The benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) opened higher but has seesawed since. It was last +0.36% at 5,294.94 points. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.08% at 16,293.69 points. 

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -1.20% at 39,193.28 points. Constituent UnitedHealth (UNH) crashed -22.1%, after the managed care giant delivered a rare quarterly bottom-line miss on soaring medical care costs. It also cut its full-year profit outlook. The performance reverberated across the health insurance sector, dragging peers such as Humana (HUM) -7.1%, Molina Healthcare (MOH) -2.9%, and Amgen (AMGN) -2.7%. 

Investors also received results from Dow 30 component American Express (AXP). All eyes will be on Netflix's (NFLX) results after the closing bell.

Aside from earnings, Fed chair Powell was once again in focus.

The central bank chief the previous day expressed concerns about the likely higher-than-anticipated effects from tariffs, including possibly putting pressure on policymakers' dual mandate of stable prices and strong employment. His hawkish comments spooked traders that were already reeling from a tech rout, leading to a steep selloff on Wall Street on Wednesday.

Trump on Thursday said Powell was "always TOO LATE AND WRONG" and that he "should have lowered Interest Rates, like the ECB, long ago." "Powell's termination cannot come fast enough!" the President added.

Politico reported that U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent had been privately underscoring that removing Powell as Fed chair would feed instability in markets, citing two people close to the White House.

"Canning the head of the central bank is a recipe for higher inflation expectations and longer term interest rates. Sad!" Renaissance Macro Research said on X (formerly Twitter). "Trump will get to leave his mark on the Fed when Powell’s term as Chair is up next year," it added.  

Over in the fixed-income markets, U.S. Treasury yields were mixed. The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) was last up 2 basis points to 4.30%. The shorter-end more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was down 1 basis point to 3.78%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

data.title: Portfolio recommendations to navigate current policy uncertainty – Société Générale
data.description:
Société Générale analysts have issued a call for investors to pivot away from traditional safe havens like the U.S. dollar (DXY) and instead embrace a “Great Rotation” toward European and Asian assets as political uncertainty intensifies.

In a research note, analysts warn that increasing tariff tensions, deteriorating corporate earnings, and liquidity risks in credit markets now require proactive portfolio adjustments.

“The crushing weight of the U.S. dollar (DXY) is becoming a real issue,” said Arthur van Slooten, Global Asset Allocation strategist at Société Générale, pointing to the dollar’s weakening trajectory despite rising Treasury yields (GOVI), (SPTL).

The bank has reduced credit exposure by 6 percentage points to 13% in its multi-asset portfolio, citing “sharp net outflows from credit funds in the U.S. and Europe” driven by tariff stress and stretched valuations.

According to the note, “The need for additional protection through credit asset swaps has diminished after recent spread widening,” though the analysts emphasized maintaining defensive positioning given ongoing volatility. This adjustment reflects deepening concerns about market stability in the current environment.

Corporate asset allocations (IGLB), (PFIG), (IGIB), (IGSB), (PHB) were dramatically cut by 11 percentage points to 53%, with equities (SP500), (DJI), (COMP:IND) reduced to a moderate underweight at 40%, van Slooten said. 

Société Générale analysts also highlighted that risky corporate assets still represent 66.1% of global portfolios – just 0.8% below historical peaks – calling current levels “quite high.”

This substantial portfolio reshuffle reflects growing concerns about “second-round effects” of trade disputes potentially eroding corporate profit margins.

In addition, excessive U.S. dollar (DXY) exposure is creating critical vulnerability, with the currency representing 82% of assets in tracked funds, analysts said. Société Générale has deliberately reduced U.S. dollar weighting by eight points to 31% in its portfolio, advocating for euro (PTEU) and yen diversification as alternatives.

European equity funds (VGK), (IEV) (IEUR), (STOXX) have experienced renewed inflows, including the strongest U.S. investor interest in two years, fueled by Germany’s fiscal stimulus and fading geopolitical risks, according to the note.

Société Générale analysts view this shift as “driven by sound fundamentals,” contrasting with their caution toward premature “bottom-fishing” in U.S. markets.

“The home bias of U.S.-based investors could become increasingly challenged,” van Slooten concluded, endorsing strategic rebalancing toward regions with clearer valuation upside and policy catalysts.

data.title: American express maintains 2025 guidance for 8%-10% revenue growth and $15-$15.50 EPS
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Express Company (AXP) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Stephen Squeri highlighted that Q1 revenues reached $17 billion, an 8% year-over-year increase on an FX-adjusted basis, with net income of $2.6 billion or $3.64 per share. He emphasized that the company's premium card member base continues to show healthy spending levels, with total card member spending up 6% during the quarter.
  • Squeri noted the addition of 3.4 million new cards, predominantly driven by Millennial and Gen-Z customers, who represent over 60% of new accounts. Card fee growth was recorded at 20% on an FX-adjusted basis.
  • CFO Christophe Le Caillec stated that the 8% FX-adjusted revenue growth aligns with the company's full-year guidance. He added that delinquency and write-off rates remain stable and below pre-pandemic levels, while card fee growth marked a 27th consecutive quarter of double-digit growth.
  • The company confirmed its commitment to investing in long-term growth areas, such as technology, product refreshes, and market expansion, while maintaining disciplined expense management.

Outlook

  • Full-year guidance for revenue growth is maintained at 8%-10%, with EPS guidance between $15 and $15.50. Management highlighted that these projections incorporate a peak unemployment rate of 5.7%.
  • Squeri noted that early Q2 spending trends mirror Q1 levels across all customer segments and spending categories.
  • Le Caillec emphasized the stability of credit performance and consumer spending trends, projecting continuity in their premium strategy's effectiveness.

Financial Results

  • Q1 revenue was $17 billion, an increase of 8% year-over-year on an FX-adjusted basis. EPS stood at $3.64.
  • Goods and services spending experienced faster growth than in 2024, while travel and entertainment (T&E) spending remained steady. Airline spending showed some deceleration but was offset by strength in restaurant and lodging billings.
  • Net card fees grew by 20%, supported by the acquisition of 3.4 million new card members, 70% of whom were enrolled in fee-paying products.
  • Net interest income increased by 11% on an FX-adjusted basis, driven by growth in revolving loans.
  • The CET1 ratio was 10.7%, and $1.3 billion was returned to shareholders, including $600 million in dividends and $700 million in share repurchases. American Express also increased its dividend by 17% during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Sanjay Sakhrani, KBW: Questioned potential revenue impacts from spending pull-forwards and expense reduction strategies. Squeri clarified there has been no significant pull-forward in consumer spending, emphasizing the company's commitment to long-term investments regardless of short-term revenue fluctuations.
  • Mark DeVries, Deutsche Bank: Asked about the impact of potential tariffs. Squeri identified small businesses as a segment potentially at risk but noted proactive risk management strategies are in place.
  • Don Fandetti, Wells Fargo: Inquired about fee growth and refresh strategies. Squeri confirmed the company's practice of aligning fee increases with added value through product refreshes.
  • Erika Najarian, UBS: Sought clarification on how the company’s unemployment assumptions are factored into guidance. Le Caillec explained that the 5.7% unemployment projection is used for credit reserve calculations and does not imply sustained levels throughout the year.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns about macroeconomic uncertainties and spending sustainability but acknowledged the company's resilience and premium customer base.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing their ability to navigate various economic scenarios while continuing to invest for long-term growth.
  • During the Q&A, management demonstrated a consistent strategy-focused approach, avoiding defensive language and emphasizing stability and flexibility across business segments.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue growth in Q1 2025 was consistent with Q4 2024 levels, maintaining an 8% FX-adjusted increase year-over-year.
  • Spending trends in goods and services accelerated, while T&E growth remained steady. Airline spending decelerated compared to Q4 2024, but strength in other areas of T&E offset this.
  • Management's tone remained confident, consistent with the previous quarter, underscoring stability in their premium strategy and long-term investment plans.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged macroeconomic uncertainties, including potential impacts from higher unemployment and tariff changes.
  • Analysts raised questions about potential spending slowdowns and the company’s ability to maintain revenue guidance amid economic challenges.
  • Squeri and Le Caillec emphasized the company's expense flexibility, strong credit performance, and ability to adjust investments as needed.

Final Takeaway

American Express demonstrated strong performance in Q1 2025, supported by steady revenue growth, robust card fee increases, and stable credit metrics. The company reaffirmed its full-year guidance and highlighted continued investments in product refreshes, technology, and market expansion. Management remains confident in navigating economic uncertainties, leveraging its premium-focused strategy to drive long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Bavarian Nordic gets ACIP recommendation for its chikungunya vaccine
data.description:
• The U.S. Centers for Disease Control's vaccine advisory committee voted to recommend the single-dose vaccine, Vimkunya, for the prevention chikungunya in 12 years and older traveling to a country experiencing an outbreak, Bavarian Nordic (BVNKF) announced.
  • The vaccine may be considered for persons traveling or taking up residence in a country or territory without an outbreak, but with elevated risk for US travelers if planning travel for an extended period of time.
  • In another voting, CDC's Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) also recommended Vimkunya for laboratory workers with potential for exposure to chikungunya virus, the company added.

data.title: JP Morgan estimates Nvidia, AMD's 2025 earnings take 8-10% hit from new restrictions
data.description:
The new licensing requirements for Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) to export certain chips to China is estimated to negatively affect these companies' earnings per share by 8% to 10% in calendar year 2025, according to an analysis by J.P. Morgan.

Nvidia and AMD received notices from the U.S. government stating they need a special license to ship their H20 and MI308 chips, respectively, to China. Nvidia said it now expects to take a charge of $5.5B due to the halt in shipments. AMD said it expects to suffer an $800M charge. 

"Assuming a 65-67% gross margin on the $5.5B inventory charge, we estimate the revenue impact is $15-$16B impact to NVDA on a total of $180B in DC revs this year, or about 8-10% of the datacenter revenue," said J.P. Morgan analysts, led by Harlan Sur, in an investor note. "We estimate the overall EPS impact is about 8-10% for this year."

AMD is expected to take a similar hit, percentage wise.

"Assuming a 45-55% gross margin on the $800M inventory charge, we estimate the revenue impact to AMD is $1.5-$1.8B on a total of $8B in GPU revenues this year and $16B in datacenter revenue, or about 10% of the datacenter revenue," Sur noted. "The EPS impact is estimated to be approximately 10% for this year. In summary, we estimate the total impact on merchant GPU (Nvidia and AMD) AI revenues this year to be around 8-10%."
